seo.title: Songkhla Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Songkhla Province: Hat Yai city, Songkhla Lake, Ko Yo island, Ton Nga Chang Waterfall, and 16 districts to explore. Area: 7,394 km², population: 1.4 million. Plan your visit.

overview: Songkhla is one of Thailand's most diverse and strategically important southern provinces, covering 7,394 km² along the Gulf of Thailand coast. While Songkhla city is the official capital, Hat Yai — the province's commercial powerhouse — is the largest city in the south with over 350,000 residents and a thriving cross-border trade hub with Malaysia. The province is famous for Songkhla Lake (the largest natural lake in Thailand), the multiethnic Hat Yai street food scene blending Thai, Chinese, and Malay influences, and the Seven-Tiered Ton Nga Chang Waterfall. Songkhla's population is a rich mix of Buddhists, Muslims, and the Chinese-Thai community, giving it a distinctly cosmopolitan character unlike anywhere else in the region.
geography: Songkhla Province occupies the lower eastern peninsula of Thailand, bordering the Gulf of Thailand to the east and sharing a southern border with Malaysia (Kedah and Perlis states). The province is bisected by Songkhla Lake, Thailand's largest natural lake at roughly 1,040 km² — actually a system of three connected lakes (Thale Luang, Thale Sap, and Thale Noi) separated from the sea by a narrow sand barrier. The terrain shifts from coastal lowlands and rubber plantations in the east to forested hills along the western border with Satun, where Ton Nga Chang Wildlife Sanctuary protects dense jungle and the province's most spectacular waterfall.
highlights: Hat Yai — the south's commercial capital and a legendary food city with night markets, dim sum, and Muslim street food
Songkhla Lake — Thailand's largest natural lake at 1,040 km², home to the Ko Yo island and the Southern Thai Folklore Museum
Ton Nga Chang Waterfall — a seven-tier waterfall in a wildlife sanctuary, 30 km west of Hat Yai
Songkhla Old Town — a well-preserved Sino-Portuguese heritage town with a famous bronze mermaid statue and hilltop temples
Wat Hat Yai Nai — home to one of the largest reclining Buddha statues in the world, 35 meters long
Phra Buddha Mongkol Maharaj — a towering 22-meter golden standing Buddha visible from across Hat Yai
Hat Yai Municipal Park with the Hat Yai Funicular — 60 THB cable car ride to hilltop Four Faced Buddha shrine
Ko Yo Island — a floating island community on Songkhla Lake known for the cotton-weaving cottage industry and seafood restaurants

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Jan–Apr: Dry season with hot sunny days (28–35°C) and the lowest rainfall of the year. The Gulf of Thailand side stays calm for coastal activities. Hat Yai's street markets and night food stalls are at their liveliest in cool January evenings.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: May–Jun and Oct–Nov: Transition months with occasional showers but manageable weather. Hat Yai's commercial district is never truly off-season thanks to year-round Malaysian visitor traffic.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jul–Sep: The wettest months — the Gulf coast receives monsoon rains from the southwest. Songkhla Lake rises and the jungle at Ton Nga Chang turns lush and green. Hotels drop 20–30% and crowds thin significantly.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit January to April for the best weather. Hat Yai rewards visitors year-round thanks to its indoor markets, air-conditioned shopping malls, and food scene that never sleeps. Avoid October if possible — the tail end of the wet season can bring flooding in low-lying areas.

topAttractions.name: Wat Hat Yai Nai (Reclining Buddha)
topAttractions.description: Home to one of the largest reclining Buddha statues in the world — Phra Phuttha Mahatta Mongkhon — measuring 35 meters long and 15 meters high. The interior is walkable, housing a meditation hall with religious relics. Located on Phet Kasem Road near the Khlong U-Tapao river in central Hat Yai.

topAttractions.name: Ko Yo Island & Southern Thai Folklore Museum
topAttractions.description: Ko Yo is a community island set in Songkhla Lake, connected to the mainland by two bridges. The Folklore Museum (Institute for Southern Thai Studies, Thaksin University) sits on a hilltop with sweeping views over the lake and displays traditional southern Thai art, crafts, and shadow puppets. Ko Yo village is also famous for its handwoven cotton fabrics and fresh lakeside seafood.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 100 THB foreigners, 50 THB Thais (~$3 USD). Open daily 8:30–17:00

topAttractions.name: Ton Nga Chang Waterfall & Wildlife Sanctuary
topAttractions.description: A dramatic seven-tiered waterfall cascading through the jungle of Ton Nga Chang Wildlife Sanctuary, approximately 30 km west of Hat Yai. The name means 'Elephant Tusk Waterfall' — the two main streams split around a rock formation resembling tusks. Each of the seven tiers is accessible on foot via jungle trail. Best visited during or just after the rainy season (Sep–Nov) when water volume is highest.

sources.sourceName: Tripadvisor — Ton Nga Chang Wildlife Sanctuary

topAttractions.name: Hat Yai Municipal Park & Funicular
topAttractions.description: A large hilltop park in the northeast of Hat Yai, popular with locals for morning exercise and evening strolls. The park's main draw is the funicular (cable car) ride to a summit shrine featuring a golden Four Faced Buddha (Brahma) image and panoramic views over the city. The park also contains a Chinese pagoda, fitness areas, and shaded walking paths.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free entry to park; Funicular 60 THB per person

topAttractions.name: Phra Buddha Mongkol Maharaj (Standing Buddha)
topAttractions.description: A monumental 22-meter-tall golden standing Buddha statue on a hilltop near the Kuan Im Temple in Hat Yai. Visible from across much of the city, it is one of Hat Yai's most photographed landmarks. The statue is surrounded by smaller shrine buildings and offers city views. Located about 3 km from the Hat Yai city center.

sources.sourceName: Lonely Planet — Phra Buddha Mongkol Maharaj

topAttractions.name: Songkhla Old Town & Bronze Mermaid
topAttractions.description: The historic heart of Songkhla city features Sino-Portuguese shophouse architecture, colorful temples, and a relaxed pace far removed from Hat Yai's commercial bustle. The famous Bronze Mermaid statue (Nang Ngam) on Samila Beach is the city's enduring symbol, overlooking the Gulf of Thailand. Nearby are Songkhla National Museum (in a restored Chinese mansion) and hilltop Wat Khao Noi with panoramic coastal views.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (Mermaid statue, Samila Beach); Songkhla National Museum 30 THB

localFood: Hat Yai Fried Chicken (Gai Tort Hat Yai) — crispy southern-style fried chicken marinated in turmeric and garlic, served with sticky rice and deep-fried shallots; a Hat Yai signature found on virtually every street corner

Dim Sum (Halal) — Hat Yai's unique halal dim sum scene blends Chinese dumplings with Malay flavors; Dimsum Chabura is the most famous spot, drawing Malaysian day-trippers specifically for this hybrid cuisine
Mataba — unleavened fried bread stuffed with spiced minced beef and egg, a Malay-Muslim specialty widely sold in Hat Yai's Muslim quarter near Kim Yong Market

Khao Yam — Southern Thai rice salad with dried shrimp, shredded coconut, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, toasted coconut, and fermented shrimp paste dressing; a breakfast staple unique to the deep south
Roti Kaeng — flaky roti flatbread served with rich southern Thai curry (gaeng tai pla or massaman), sold at Muslim street stalls from early morning
Ko Yo Seafood — fresh fish, prawns, and squid grilled or steamed at the Ko Yo lakeside restaurants; the island's own smoked fish (pla ra) is a prized local condiment
Nasi Kerabu — blue-rice Malay dish (rice cooked with butterfly pea flowers) served with grilled chicken, pickled vegetables, and coconut sambal; common in Hat Yai's halal eateries
Bird's Nest Soup — Hat Yai has one of Thailand's largest communities of swiftlet-nest harvesters; restaurants around Suntisook Market serve authentic bird's nest soup at far lower prices than Bangkok
culture: Songkhla Province is one of the most culturally layered provinces in Thailand, shaped by centuries of Malay, Chinese, and Thai Buddhist influences. Hat Yai functions as the commercial capital of the Thai south and draws millions of Malaysian shoppers annually — giving the city a bilingual Malay-Thai street culture rare anywhere else in Thailand. The province observes both Thai Buddhist festivals (Loy Krathong on Songkhla Lake is particularly beautiful) and significant Muslim holidays. Songkhla's Old Town preserves Sino-Portuguese architecture and fishing village culture, while Ko Yo maintains traditional southern Thai crafts including handwoven phaa ko yo cotton fabric, a GI-protected textile. Shadow puppet theater (Nang Talung) originated in the deep south and the Songkhla region is considered its heartland.
gettingThere: Hat Yai International Airport (HDY) is 12 km from the city center and served by Nok Air, Thai AirAsia, Thai Lion Air, and Thai VietJet from Bangkok Don Mueang (roughly 1.5 hours, from ~700 THB). Overnight trains from Bangkok's Krung Thep Aphiwat Station reach Hat Yai Railway Station in 15–17 hours (sleeper from ~500 THB). Long-distance buses from Bangkok's Southern Bus Terminal take 13–16 hours (from ~450 THB). Hat Yai is also a major border crossing hub — Padang Besar (40 km south) connects directly to Penang and Kuala Lumpur by train and bus, making Songkhla a key entry point for overland travelers from Malaysia.
gettingAround: Hat Yai city is compact enough to walk between major attractions in the center. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) run fixed routes within Hat Yai for 10–20 THB. Grab ride-hailing is active in Hat Yai and is the most convenient option for door-to-door travel (~60–120 THB for most city trips). Renting a motorbike (250–350 THB/day) is the best way to reach Ton Nga Chang Waterfall (30 km west) and the outskirts. For Ko Yo Island and Songkhla city (28 km from Hat Yai), songthaews and local buses depart from the Hat Yai bus terminal, or charter a taxi for ~400–600 THB return. Tuk-tuks are common for short hops; agree on the price before boarding.
travelTips: Hat Yai's Kim Yong Market and Suntisook Market are the best spots for cheap street food — go hungry and graze between stalls
Malaysian ringgit is widely accepted in Hat Yai shops and markets — no need to exchange all your cash before crossing the border
The Hat Yai Saturday Night Market in front of Odean Shopping Centre is one of the best night markets in the south — don't miss it
Book trains well in advance, especially for sleeper cars on the Bangkok–Hat Yai route; they sell out weeks ahead during Thai holidays
Ton Nga Chang Waterfall is only worth visiting from September to May — the waterfall can slow to a trickle during the dry peak of April
Songkhla Old Town is a 30-minute drive from Hat Yai but feels like a different world — allocate a full half-day for the mermaid statue, museum, and old temple walk
Hat Yai has a large Muslim community — many restaurants close during Friday midday prayers; plan lunch accordingly near the center
The Ko Yo Folklore Museum closes on Mondays — combine the trip with a lakeside seafood lunch on the island
